28: deute pros me pantes hoi kopionte kai pephortimenoi — come to me all who labor and are heavy laden — kopionto (laboring to exhaustion) + pephortimenoi (perfect passive, permanently burdened) describes the condition; deute pros me (come to me) is Wisdom's invitation. anapausis — rest, refreshment — not idleness but the rest of the Sabbath: cessation from the burden of self-justifying religious effort. chrestos (Matthew 11:30) — good, pleasant, kind — the same word used for good wine that has aged well (Luke 5:39) and for the kindness of God (Romans 2:4); the yoke of Christ has the quality of what has been made right.
1: kai egeneto hote etelesen ho Iēsous — and it happened when Jesus had finished (etelesen aorist of teleō: to complete, bring to an end) — this formula closes each of Matthew's five major discourse blocks (Matthew 7:28; Matthew 11:1; Matthew 13:53; Matthew 19:1; Matthew 26:1). Metebē ekeithen tou didaskein kai kēryssein en tais polesin autōn — he departed from there to teach and proclaim in their cities: the discourse on mission (Matthew 10) leads directly to missionary practice.
2: ho de Iōannēs akousas en tō desmōtēriō ta erga tou Christou — John, hearing in prison the works of the Messiah (ta erga tou Christou: the deeds of the Christ — Matthew uses Christou here without the article, as a title) — desmōtērion (prison, place of binding): John is imprisoned by Herod Antipas (Matthew 14:3), setting up the contrast between his confinement and the liberation Jesus is enacting through the miracle-list of Matthew 11:5.
3: su ei ho erchomenos ē heteron prosdokōmen — are you the coming one or should we expect another (ho erchomenos, the coming one — a technical title for the eschatological deliverer, rooted in Psalms 118:26; Malachi 3:1) — the question is not doubt but identification: John wants to know whether Jesus's ministry matches the coming one he proclaimed (Matthew 3:11). Prosdokōmen present subjunctive: are we to keep waiting/expecting.
4: poreuentes apangeilate Iōannē ha akouete kai blepete — go and report to John what you hear and see — the format echoes the prophetic pattern of Isaiah 29:18-19; Matthew 35:5-6; Matthew 61:1, where the messianic deliverer is identified by what he does rather than what he claims. The answer is exegetically dense: it presupposes John knows the prophetic texts and will recognize the list as the messianic inventory.
5: typhloi anablepousin kai chōloi peripatousin — the blind receive sight and the lame walk — the six-item list (blind, lame, lepers, deaf, dead, poor) maps precisely onto Isaiah 35:5-6 (blind/deaf/lame) + Isaiah 61:1 (poor have good news proclaimed). Nekroi egeirontai (dead are raised): inserted between the deaf and the poor, surpassing both Isaiah sources. Ptōchoi euangelizontai — the poor have good news proclaimed (euangelizō passive: the same verb as Isaiah 61:1 LXX; the proclamation is itself the messianic act).
6: makarios estin hos ean mē skandalisthē en emoi — blessed is whoever does not stumble on account of me (skandalisthē aorist passive subjunctive of skandalizō, the passive: "not be caused to stumble") — the Beatitude form applied not to a virtue but to a response to Jesus himself: the potential stumbling-block is his identity. The statement implicitly acknowledges that some do find him a stumbling-block — the response to his deeds divides (cf. Isaiah 8:14; 1 Peter 2:8).
7: ti exēlthate eis tēn erēmon theasasthai — what did you go out into the wilderness to see (theasasthai aorist infinitive of theaomai, to gaze at, observe as a spectacle — a stronger, more deliberate looking than blepō) — kalamon hypo anemou saleuomenon (a reed being shaken by the wind): the question-and-dismissal format of Matthew 11:7-9 is rhetorical, eliminating two wrong answers before arriving at the right one. A wind-blown reed is a figure of fickleness; the crowd did not go out for something trivially unstable.
8: anthrōpon en malakois hēmphiesmenon — a man clothed in soft/delicate garments (malakos, soft — used of clothing to mean fine, luxurious; used of persons to mean morally soft) — idou hoi ta malaka phorountes en tois oikois tōn basileōn eisin (those wearing soft things are in the houses of kings): John's clothing (camel hair, leather belt — Matthew 3:4) is the antithesis of malaka. The contrast locates John in the prophetic tradition, not the royal court.
9: alla ti exēlthate idein, prophētēn — but what did you go out to see, a prophet? — nai legō hymin kai perissoteron prophētou (yes I tell you and more than a prophet: perissoteron comparative of perissos, exceeding): the double affirmation acknowledges John as prophet and then immediately surpasses the category. What makes John more than a prophet is revealed in Matthew 11:10 — he is the messenger long expected.
10: houtos estin peri hou gegraptai — this is the one about whom it is written (gegraptai perfect passive: it stands written, the perfect marking enduring scriptural authority) — the citation is a composite of Exodus 23:20 LXX (idou apostellō ton angelon mou) and Malachi 3:1 (pros kataskeuasai tēn hodon sou emprosthen sou). Matthew shifts Malachi's "before me" to "before you," applying the divine messenger-sending directly to Jesus. The combination identifies John as both the Exodus wilderness-guide and the Malachi forerunner.
11: en gennētois gynaikōn — among those born of women (gennētos gynaikōn, a Semitic idiom for every human being, emphasizing creaturely humanity; cf. Job 14:1) — meizōn Iōannou tou baptistou oudeis egēgertai (no one greater has arisen — egēgertai perfect passive: has been raised up, by divine agency). Ho de mikroteros en tē basileia tōn ouranōn meizōn autou estin — the lesser in the kingdom of the heavens is greater than he: mikroteros comparative; the kingdom establishes a different greatness-scale than birth-among-women.
12: hē basileia tōn ouranōn biazetai — the kingdom of the heavens is subjected to violence / forces its way / presses forward (biazetai: middle or passive of biazō, disputed; middle = "is advancing forcefully"; passive = "is being violated/seized") — kai biastai harpazousin autēn (and violent people are seizing it — harpazō, to seize, snatch): the verse is among Matthew's most debated. The range runs from "the kingdom forcefully advances and the forceful lay hold of it" (positive reading) to "the kingdom suffers violence and violent men assault it" (negative, applied to Herod/opposition to John/Jesus).
13: pantes gar hoi prophētai kai ho nomos heōs Iōannou eprophēteusan — for all the Prophets and the Law prophesied until John — the unusual order (Prophets before Law) may be deliberate emphasis; the standard phrase was "the Law and the Prophets" (Matthew 5:17; Matthew 7:12). Heōs Iōannou (until John): John marks the terminus of the prophetic age. This supports Matthew 11:10 — John is not just a prophet but the endpoint of prophetic anticipation, beyond which the thing anticipated has arrived.
14: kai ei thelete dexasthai — and if you are willing to receive it (thelete present: if you are presently willing — a conditional on receptivity) — autos estin Ēlias ho mellōn erchesthai (he himself is Elijah who is about to come). The identification is conditional: only for those willing to receive it. John does not share Elijah's manner but fills the Elijah function: a voice calling Israel to repentance before the day of the LORD (Malachi 4:5).
15: ho echōn ōta akouetō — the one having ears, let them hear — a formulaic call to attention (akouetō present imperative: begin/continue hearing), used in Matthew after weighty or paradoxical sayings (Matthew 13:9,43). Its placement after the Elijah identification implies that this identification is hidden to casual listening but available to those who press into the implications of the prophetic texts.
16: tini de homoiōsō tēn genean tautēn — to what shall I compare this generation (homoiōsō aorist subjunctive: the deliberative use) — hē genea autē (this generation): the term carries a moral/spiritual load in Matthew; it is the generation characterized by resistance to the kingdom. The comparison deploys a children's game parable: the generation refuses every mode of engagement — neither the celebratory invitation nor the penitential one.
17: ēulēsamen hymin kai ouk ōrchēsasthe — we played the pipe for you and you did not dance — ēulēsamen aorist of auleō (to play the flute, a wedding/celebration instrument); ōrchēsasthe aorist of orcheomai (to dance): the wedding scenario. Ethrēnēsamen kai ouk ekopsasthe (we sang a dirge and you did not mourn — thrēneō, to sing a funeral lament; koptomai, to beat one's breast in mourning): the funeral scenario. Both modes are refused. The parable is about the generation's refusal to engage with either John or Jesus on any terms.
18: ēlthen gar Iōannēs mēte esthiōn mēte pinōn — for John came neither eating nor drinking — the double mēte...mēte marks absolute abstinence: John's ascetic practice (Matthew 3:4; Matthew 9:14) was extreme even by the standards of fasting disciples. Kai legousin daimonion echei (and they say: he has a demon): the accusation against John takes the form of a demonization of his abstinence — his refusal of normal social-food is explained as demonic possession rather than prophetic calling.
19: ēlthen ho huios tou anthrōpou esthiōn kai pinōn — the Son of Man came eating and drinking — the contrast with John (Matthew 11:18) is total: Jesus participates in normal meals and social eating. Idou anthrōpos phagos kai oinopotēs (Look, a glutton and a wine-drinker — phagos, glutton; oinopotēs, wine-drinker/drunkard): the slanders are the inverse of John's charge. Edikaioōthē hē sophia apo tōn ergōn autēs — wisdom was justified/vindicated by her deeds (edikaioōthē aorist passive: stands justified; Luke has "by her children"): the deeds of Jesus and John together are wisdom's self-vindication.
20: tote ērxato oneidizein tas poleis — then he began to denounce the cities (oneidizō, to reproach, upbraid — a strong word for public condemnation; used of mocking Jesus in Matthew 27:44) — en hais egenonto hai pleistai dynameis autou (in which most of his miracles had happened — dynameis, powers, mighty works): the denunciation is grounded in the specific history of miraculous witness. Greater exposure produces greater accountability; the woes follow the logic of Matthew 11:21-24.
21: ouai soi, Chorazin, ouai soi, Bēthsaida — woe to you, Chorazin; woe to you, Bethsaida — ouai (woe) in prophetic discourse signals divine grief-and-judgment (cf. Isaiah 5:8-22). Chorazin and Bethsaida are not mentioned elsewhere as miracle-sites in Matthew, suggesting a wider tradition beyond what the Gospels record. Tyrō kai Sidōni (Tyre and Sidon): notorious Gentile cities condemned by the prophets (Isa 23; Ezek 26-28). That they would have repented in sackcloth and ashes (sakkō kai spodō, formal mourning posture) makes the Galilean failure more acute.
22: plēn legō hymin Tyrō kai Sidōni anektoteron estai en hēmera kriseōs ē hymin — but I tell you, for Tyre and Sidon it will be more tolerable on the day of judgment than for you — plēn (but, however: a strong adversative); anektoteron (more bearable/tolerable: comparative, establishing a graduated scale of judgment). The judgment on Galilee is not the absence of judgment but its heightened form: having received more, more is required (cf. Luke 12:48).
23: kai sy, Kapharnaoun, mē heōs ouranou hypsōthēsē — and you, Capernaum, will you be lifted to the heavens? (hypsōthēsē future passive of hypsoō) — heōs haidou katabēsē (you will descend to Hades — katabēsē future): the contrast echoes Isaiah 14:13-15 (the king of Babylon's boast of heavenly ascent followed by descent to Sheol). The allusion frames Capernaum's pride in its messianic privileges as Babel-arrogance. Hoti ei en Sodomois egenēthēsan hai dynameis — for if the miracles done in you had been done in Sodom, it would have remained to this day.
24: plēn legō hymin hoti gē Sodomōn anektoteron estai en hēmera kriseōs ē soi — but I tell you that the land of Sodom will be more tolerable on judgment day than for you — Sodom, the paradigmatic city of destruction in Genesis 19, becomes the reference point by which Capernaum's guilt is measured. The logic mirrors Matthew 11:21-22: Gentile cities famous for wickedness face lighter judgment than Israelite cities that received the full witness of messianic miracles and rejected them.
25: exomologoumai soi pater kyrie tou ouranou kai tēs gēs — I confess/praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth (exomologeomai: to acknowledge publicly, confess, or give praise; the word carries both confession and doxology) — hoti apekrypsas tauta apo sophōn kai synetōn kai apekalypsas auta nēpiois (because you hid these things from the wise and understanding and revealed them to little children — nēpioi: infants/little ones, not the spiritually mature but the socially insignificant): the prayer is Jesus's theological response to the cities' rejection — the pattern of divine concealment-and-revelation explains what has just happened.
26: nai ho patēr hoti houtōs eudokia egeneto emprosthen sou — yes, Father, because such was your good pleasure before you (eudokia, good pleasure, gracious purpose — from eudokeō, to be well-pleased; the same root as the voice at the baptism Matthew 3:17) — the verse repeats and affirms Matthew 11:25 as a doxology. Emprosthen sou (before you, in your sight): the divine decision to conceal and reveal is not arbitrary but the expression of the Father's gracious pleasure.
27: panta moi paredothē hypo tou patros mou — all things have been delivered to me by my Father (paredothē aorist passive of paradidōmi: to hand over, entrust — the same verb for handing over prisoners but here for positive transfer of authority) — kai oudeis epiginōskei ton huion ei mē ho patēr (and no one fully knows the Son except the Father — epiginōskō: intensive knowing, relational recognition). Oude ton patera tis epiginōskei ei mē ho huios kai hō ean boulētai ho huios apokalypsai — nor does anyone know the Father except the Son and whoever the Son chooses to reveal him to. This verse, often called the "Johannine thunderbolt," is the synoptic high-Christological statement: mutual, exclusive, intensive knowledge between Father and Son.
29: arate ton zygon mou eph hymas kai mathete ap emou — take my yoke upon you and learn from me — arate aorist imperative of airō; zygon (yoke) is both the literal farming yoke and the rabbinic metaphor for Torah-obedience: "taking the yoke of the kingdom." Mathete ap emou (learn from me — manthanō aorist imperative: the root of mathētēs/disciple): Jesus invites discipleship to himself as teacher. Hoti praus eimi kai tapeinos tē kardia — for I am gentle and humble in heart: the only place in the Gospels where Jesus describes his own character in these specific terms. Praus (meek/gentle — the third Beatitude Matthew 5:5) and tapeinos tē kardia (humble in heart, not merely external humility).
30: ho gar zygos mou chrēstos — for my yoke is kind/good (chrēstos: kind, gracious, pleasant, good in quality — the word used in 1 Peter 2:3 of God's goodness, quoting Psalms 34:8; not merely "easy" in the sense of requiring little effort but "gracious" in character) — kai to phortion mou elaphron estin (and my burden is light — elaphron, light in weight; phortion, burden, load): the contrast with the Pharisees' "heavy burdens" of 23:4 is implicit. The chrēstos/elaphron pair defines the quality of Jesus's yoke, not merely its quantity of demands.
